change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew 421→1501 chars (+256.5%); detail_evidence_length grew 872→1493 (+71.2%); pre-tldr-schema fingerprint, compared on common fields only; sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-09T01:10:03Z; obe_skipped=1 (WIN-063 was head of list but already re-reviewed via WIN-063.c3 at 2026-05-09T02:44Z, post-audit). The new evidence text adds a quantitative dome-geometry over-prediction argument (~2.3:1 inner/outer flux ratio → 90–270 W/m² range); the new verdict text adds a substantial post-hoc-test analysis tying WIN-066 to Part 3.1b's commit-timestamp framework and noting that the dome has no monitor.py entry for the value, so even the dome author's own weaker timestamp test is inapplicable.
our_argument_summary: The +0.34 W/m² figure the dome cites as a static hemispheric NH excess is in fact a decadal trend in NH-minus-SH absorbed solar radiation reported by satellite (Loeb et al. 2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122; the relevant baseline-symmetry result is Stephens et al. 2012, Nature Geoscience, doi:10.1038/ngeo1580). The dome provides no radiation-budget derivation that would yield any specific W/m² value from dome geometry — the value is post-hoc-adopted from CERES literature. Moreover, if the dome attempted such a derivation, inverse-cube flux falloff on a flat disc with a local sun at h=5,733 km predicts an inner/outer flux asymmetry of order 2:1–6:1 depending on the sun's orbital radius, which would produce an NH-SH difference of order 90–270 W/m² — hundreds of times larger than observed. The verdict text additionally invokes Part 3.1b: no monitor.py entry exists for this value, so even the author's weaker commit-timestamp version of his own post-hoc test cannot be applied. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/02b-wins-V51-win064.txt (V51 dome page captured for WIN-064 only — does not include WIN-066 prose)
no monitor.py / pull_data.py captured in the current raw-text/ snapshot
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No dome-side calculation pipeline exists for WIN-066. There is no energy-budget script, no CERES-data pull, no hemispheric-comparison logic. The dome simply asserts that geometric asymmetry 'predicts' a +0.34 W/m² NH excess and labels the published CERES-derived value as confirmation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no validation logic of any kind exists for this WIN, so neither hardcoded comparison nor live fetch applies.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None observed. Aetheric refraction is not invoked for the energy-budget claim.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the code_analysis tag monitoring=none is correct, and our review's prose does not over-claim about dome automation for this WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No dome monitor file in the current raw-text/ snapshot references WIN-066, hemispheric energy, NH/SH absorbed solar radiation, or CERES. The c2 review confirmed monitoring=none against a fuller raw-text snapshot that included monitor.py and pull_data.py; the current snapshot is sparser (V51 page captured WIN-064 only) but provides no contradicting evidence. The dome's V51 wins page lists WIN-066 in the WIN catalogue without any associated automated check.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hemispheric energy balance is quantified by CERES satellite data and analyzed by published climate science. Stephens et al. 2012 established baseline near-symmetry; Loeb et al. 2025 (the actual authors of the paper at doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122) document the emerging decadal trend. The dome contributes no new numerical prediction — it adopts the published asymmetry trend value (0.34 W/m²/decade) and reframes it as confirmation of geometric asymmetry without changing the number.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The +0.34 W/m²/decade hemispheric ASR trend was published in Loeb, Thorsen, Kato, Rose, Hodnebrog 2025 (PNAS, online 2025-09-29) — well before dome V51.0 (2026). It is indexed in CrossRef and OpenAlex, available to any scanning agent. The dome adopts this number wholesale and labels it confirmed.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome formula starts from disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, or any other dome parameter and arrives at a hemispheric flux value. The dome page asserts that Ovoid Tilt geometry 'predicts' an NH excess, but provides no flux integral, no inverse-square or inverse-cube falloff calculation, and no temperature/albedo coupling. As shown in our quantitative_verification field below, if the derivation were attempted from dome parameters, the result would be 90–270 W/m² — orders of magnitude larger than the observed 0.34 W/m²/decade trend.

holes_found.description: WRONG-AUTHOR CITATION. Both detail_evidence and our internal issue history (ISS-065, ISS-285) attribute the +0.34 W/m²/decade figure to 'Raghuraman et al. (2025), PNAS' at doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122. Verified via CrossRef and OpenAlex (queried 2026-05-09): the paper at that exact DOI is 'Emerging hemispheric asymmetry of Earth's radiation' by Loeb, Thorsen, Kato, Rose, and Hodnebrog (PNAS, online 2025-09-29; print 2025-10-07; issue 40). The abstract verbatim reports a 'northern hemisphere (NH) minus southern hemisphere (SH) trend difference of 0.34 ± 0.23 W m^-2 dec^-1 (5 to 95% CI) in absorbed solar radiation' — i.e., the exact figure we cite, but the author attribution is wrong. There is a real Raghuraman et al. 2021 PNAS paper on Earth's energy imbalance trend (doi:10.1073/pnas.2113302118), which is plausibly how the misattribution got introduced — but for THIS DOI and THIS hemispheric figure, the lead author is Loeb. A dome defender who clicks the link sees Loeb on the masthead, not Raghuraman, and gets a free 'your review can't cite correctly' shot. This needs to be fixed everywhere, not just in detail_evidence.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'Raghuraman et al., 2025, PNAS' with 'Loeb et al., 2025, PNAS' in detail_evidence. Cycle through any other surface that carries the misattribution: open issues, closed issues (ISS-065, ISS-285 record 'Raghuraman' as the authority), prior curmudgeon reviews (WIN-066.c2.json post-hoc_evidence, EXP-285-proposal.json), and any analyst or decider notes. The DOI is correct; only the author string is wrong. Suggested replacement sentence in detail_evidence: 'The +0.34 W/m² figure cited by the dome is a decadal trend in absorbed-solar-radiation hemispheric asymmetry (Loeb et al., 2025, PNAS; doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122), driven by aerosol changes, Arctic ice loss, and cloud shifts — not a static hemispheric difference.'

holes_found.description: TLDR/DETAIL TENSION on the dome's geometric capability. tldr_evidence ends with 'The dome's geometry (concentrated energy near the center, less at the rim) can't produce a hemisphere-dependent asymmetry,' and tldr_verdict says 'The dome's axially symmetric geometry can't produce hemisphere-dependent effects.' But detail_evidence explicitly computes that under the natural inner=NH / outer=SH mapping, the dome WOULD produce a flux difference of 90–270 W/m² — three orders of magnitude too large. So the strongest argument (over-prediction by ~250–800×) is invisible to a TLDR-only reader, who instead sees a weaker and arguably wrong claim that the dome 'can't produce' any asymmetry. A motivated dome defender will read the TLDR and say 'yes it can, you just admitted in detail it produces 90–270 W/m²' — turning our own evidence against us. Per the dispatcher's TLDR severity rule, a TLDR that contradicts the expanded content it summarizes is major; this case is moderate because the TLDR is defensible as a statement about TRUE hemispheric (not center-rim) effects, but readers won't hear that nuance.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the TLDR closer in spirit: 'The dome's axially symmetric geometry produces a center-rim flux gradient, not a hemispheric one; if you map the inner half to NH and the outer half to SH, the dome predicts an NH-SH flux difference hundreds of times larger than observed.' This brings the strongest-argument punchline into the TLDR rather than burying it.

holes_found.description: RANGE-WIDTH UNDERIVED IN PROSE. detail_evidence states '~2.3× the solar energy of the outer half' and 'on the order of 90–270 W/m²' but provides no derivation of why the range is 90–270 rather than a single value. Direct quantitative check (this review): integrating the inverse-cube flux F = F0·h/(r²+h²)^1.5 over a disc of R=20,015 km with h=5,733 km gives an inner/outer flux ratio that depends strongly on the sun's orbital radius rs: rs=4,000 → 5.5:1; rs=8,000 → 3.7:1; rs=10,000 → 2.7:1; rs=20,000 → ~1.5:1. The 2.3:1 figure is consistent with rs near 11,000 km. Mapping ratio→NH-SH flux difference (assuming global mean 240 W/m²): 1.5:1 → 96 W/m²; 2.3:1 → 189 W/m²; 3.0:1 → 240 W/m²; 6.0:1 → 343 W/m². So the 90–270 range is roughly consistent with a sun-orbit-radius range of ~8,000–18,000 km — a plausibly large range over the seasonal cycle, but the prose doesn't explain that's where the range comes from, and the c2 review's stronger_argument computed a single 93 W/m² number (under a 'NH excess above mean' framing, which is half of NH-SH). A dome defender could ask why the range exists, and we should either derive it briefly or cite the controlling parameter (seasonal sun orbit radius).
holes_found.recommendation: Add one explanatory clause to detail_evidence after '90–270 W/m²': 'depending on the sun's orbital radius over the seasonal cycle.' Or, more rigorous: replace the range with a single representative value at the dome's stated solstice configuration and note that off-solstice values are even larger. This puts the responsibility on the dome's own model parameters.

holes_found.description: DIRECTION-MATCH IS NON-DISCRIMINATING. Our text rebuts the dome on magnitude (orders of magnitude wrong) but does not call out that the SIGN (NH > SH) is also predicted by mainstream science (more NH land mass → lower albedo → NH absorbs more), so 'dome predicts the right direction' is not evidence in the dome's favor. A dome advocate will say 'at least we got the sign right' and our text doesn't preempt this. The same trap exists in WIN-058/-059/-060 (NMP/SAA drift) where direction-matching is also non-discriminating. Severity is minor because magnitude-failure already kills the WIN, but a single preemptive sentence would close the loophole.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_verdict_text: 'And the direction of the asymmetry (NH > SH) is independently predicted by standard climate science from differential land/ocean distribution and aerosol forcing — so even getting the sign right is not discriminating evidence for dome geometry over the standard model.'

stronger_arguments.description: Replace 'Raghuraman' with 'Loeb' across the citation chain (see hole #1). This is not a stronger argument so much as a correctness fix, but until it's done the WIN-066 page hands a dome defender a free 'they can't even cite their sources' shot. Closing it returns us to a robust position.
sources: CrossRef API: api.crossref.org/works/10.1073/pnas.2511595122 returns Loeb et al. 2025
OpenAlex: api.openalex.org/works/doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 returns Loeb (first), Thorsen, Kato, Rose, Hodnebrog

stronger_arguments.description: The Loeb 2025 paper actually strengthens our argument beyond what the current text uses. Its abstract attributes the emerging NH-SH ASR asymmetry to 'changes in hemispheric differences in aerosol-radiation interactions, surface albedo, and water vapor changes' — i.e., entirely time-varying anthropogenic and ice-loss effects, on a 24-year baseline. A static dome geometry has zero way to produce a 24-year trend; the dome would have to predict a constant value that has been there since the model was built. So the dome's 'persistent' framing is doubly wrong: the value is not static, and the dome geometry is not capable of producing a trend.
sources: Loeb et al. 2025 abstract: 'changes in hemispheric differences in aerosol-radiation interactions, surface albedo, and water vapor changes... break in hemispheric symmetry... is linked with the atmosphere–ocean general circulation.'

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-WIN consistency leverage: WIN-067 (Antarctic gravity hole) is verdicted Self-Contradicted because dome H(r) under monotonic g↔H coupling predicts ≥38% gravity drop at the rim. WIN-066 makes the same kind of center-rim-gradient argument (inverse-cube flux falloff) but is verdicted Std Model Explains. The two could be unified: same disc geometry, same kind of failure. Promoting WIN-066 to Self-Contradicted is defensible if we adopt the implicit derivation as 'what the dome must predict if it has any radiation budget at all.' Out of scope for this review (verdict-taxonomy decision), but worth flagging to the analyst/decider for cross-cutting argument coherence.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies a real and recent finding in Earth-science literature (Loeb et al. 2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122): an emerging NH-minus-SH trend in absorbed solar radiation of 0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade. The figure is real, the satellite (CERES) data are robust, and the dome's intuition that 'an asymmetric Earth-system geometry should produce some hemispheric asymmetry' is not crazy on its face. The dome geometry is also genuinely asymmetric (NP at center vs. ice rim at the edge), so a center-rim flux gradient really does exist — the dome isn't inventing an asymmetry that has no geometric basis.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three independent failures stack: (1) zero derivation from dome parameters (data/wins.json:066.detail_evidence) — the +0.34 value is post-hoc adopted, with no flux integral on the dome site (post_hoc=true tag in code_analysis); (2) if a dome derivation were attempted, inverse-cube falloff on a flat disc with a local sun gives an NH-SH difference of order 90–270 W/m² (this review's quantitative_verification), 250–800× larger than observed — a wrong-magnitude prediction is worse than no prediction; (3) the observation is a 24-year trend driven by time-varying aerosol, albedo, and water-vapor changes (Loeb et al. 2025 abstract), and a static dome geometry has no mechanism to produce a temporal trend at all. The 'persistent NH excess' framing on the dome page is doubly wrong: not persistent, not a static feature, not derivable from dome geometry without contradicting either the magnitude or the trend.

deeper_analysis: Cycle 3 finds the substantial cycle-2→cycle-3 rewrite has incorporated all four c2 recommendations correctly: the dome-geometry over-prediction argument is in detail_evidence (c2 hole #1 fixed); detail_claim now matches the dome's 'persistent static excess' framing while noting the trend reality (c2 hole #2 fixed); the Stephens & L'Ecuyer 2015 reference has been removed and only Stephens et al. 2012 remains, eliminating the inconsistent-citation issue (c2 hole #3 fixed); a substantial new post-hoc-test paragraph in detail_verdict_text ties this WIN to Part 3.1b's commit-timestamp framework (a meaningful structural integration that goes beyond what c2 asked for). However, c2 hole #4 (PNAS DOI verification) was punted — c2 marked it 'cannot be independently confirmed' due to the publisher's Cloudflare challenge, and the cycle-3 rewrite did not return to verify. This review verifies the DOI via CrossRef and OpenAlex and surfaces a critical finding the c2 reviewer did not catch: the DOI is correct but the cited author is wrong (Loeb, not Raghuraman). The misattribution propagates through ISS-065, ISS-285, EXP-285-proposal, and WIN-066.c2.json. Recommended_action is minor_edit because the fix is a string substitution and a TLDR rephrase; the verdict and the core argument are sound.

citations_verified: Stephens et al. (2012, Nature Geoscience) doi:10.1038/ngeo1580 — DOI redirects correctly; well-known 'An update on Earth's energy balance in light of the latest global observations.'

doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 — DOI is valid AND resolves to a paper genuinely about the cited topic (NH-SH asymmetry), but the author attribution in our text is WRONG: paper is by Loeb, Thorsen, Kato, Rose, Hodnebrog 2025 — not Raghuraman as our text says. CrossRef and OpenAlex both confirm. Abstract verbatim mentions '0.34 ± 0.23 W m^-2 dec^-1' for NH-SH ASR trend difference.
citations_failed: 'Raghuraman et al., 2025, PNAS' attribution at doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 — author string does not match the paper. Likely confusion with the real Raghuraman et al. 2021 PNAS paper on Earth's energy imbalance (doi:10.1073/pnas.2113302118), which is a different paper on a related topic by a different first author. Critical fix needed.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender willing to engage on our own terms might say: 'You concede the dome geometry IS asymmetric and that a center-rim flux gradient genuinely exists on a flat disc with a local sun. You concede the SIGN of the asymmetry (NH absorbs more) is what the dome would predict if you mapped inner half to NH. The fact that this 24-year trend is now emerging in CERES data is exactly the directional confirmation the dome predicted — and the magnitude critique is straw-manning, because the dome never claimed dimensional pure-flux asymmetry. The dome model has aetheric corrections that damp the geometric flux gradient, just as it has aetheric corrections elsewhere. So the right comparison is not the bare 90-270 W/m² geometric prediction but the aether-damped residual, which the dome could fit to any observed value. The honest summary is: dome predicts hemispheric asymmetry will exist and will favor the NH; CERES now shows that. Standard models predict the same direction from independent reasoning, so this is non-discriminating — but it is also not refuting.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a paragraph to detail_evidence acknowledging two things: (1) direction-matching (NH > SH) is independently predicted by mainstream landmass/aerosol arguments, so it is non-discriminating — neither side gets credit for the sign alone; (2) any dome-side rescue via 'aetheric damping that adjusts the geometric flux gradient to fit the observed value' is unfalsifiable post-hoc fitting — there is no aether-damping formula on the dome site that derives 0.34 W/m² from dome parameters before the data, so the rescue is exactly the kind of just-so adjustment Part 3.1b warns against. The dome's geometry is being asked to be specific (90-270 W/m²) and vague (whatever the data shows) at the same time.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-066 ('Std Model Explains') and WIN-067 ('Self-Contradicted') both turn on the same dome disc-geometry argument (center-rim gradient on a flat disc with a local source). WIN-067 promotes the gradient argument to Self-Contradicted because the dome's own H(r) supplies the math. WIN-066 stays at Std Model Explains because the dome doesn't supply a radiation-budget H(r) analog. This is consistent with our verdict taxonomy (Self-Contradicted requires the dome's own equations to do the contradicting), but it does mean a sympathetic reader could compile the two into 'your review uses the same disc-gradient argument to land different verdicts'. The honest answer is 'yes, because the dome supplies its own gravity equations but not its own radiation-budget equations,' and a one-liner cross-reference between WIN-066 and WIN-067 in either prose would tighten this. WIN-056 (uses globe's 23.45° axial tilt) provides separate evidence that the dome silently borrows globe-symmetric formulas where convenient — which adds force to the WIN-066 argument that the dome can't have it both ways (axially symmetric for solar geometry but inner-outer asymmetric for hemispheric energy). No outright contradiction; flagged for the analyst as a candidate for a cross-WIN integration note.

claims_checked: The current text's claim that inner half receives ~2.3× the solar energy of outer half on dome geometry.
The current text's claim that the dome geometry would predict an NH-SH hemispheric flux difference 'on the order of 90–270 W/m²'.
Verification of doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 author and figure (CrossRef + OpenAlex).
quantitative_verification.computations: Numerical integration in Python: F(r) = F0·h/((r-rs·cosφ)²+(rs·sinφ)²+h²)^1.5 averaged over sun azimuth φ, with h=5,733 km and disc radius R=20,015 km. Inner half = disc area for r ≤ R/√2 = 14,153 km; outer half = R/√2 < r ≤ R. Inner/outer flux ratio depends on sun orbital radius rs: rs=4,000 → 5.5:1; rs=8,000 → 3.7:1; rs=10,000 → 2.7:1; rs=20,000 → ~1.5:1. The text's 2.3:1 corresponds to rs≈11,000 km. Mapping ratio→NH-SH difference (assuming global mean 240 W/m² absorbed): ratio 1.5 → 96 W/m²; ratio 2.3 → 189 W/m²; ratio 3.0 → 240 W/m²; ratio 6 → 343 W/m². So the prose's 90–270 W/m² range is internally consistent with a sun-orbit-radius range of ~8,000–18,000 km, but the prose doesn't explain that's where it comes from. CrossRef and OpenAlex confirm doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 is Loeb et al. 2025 (not Raghuraman); abstract reports '0.34 ± 0.23 W m^-2 dec^-1' for NH-SH ASR trend.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) Author attribution: 'Raghuraman' in our text vs 'Loeb' in the actual paper at the cited DOI — critical hole #1 above. (2) Range 90–270 W/m² is defensible but the lower bound 90 implies sun-orbit-radius ≈18,000 km (close to disc rim), which is physically marginal for the dome's stated solstice values; if the dome's seasonal sun orbit is, say, 4,000–10,000 km, the range should be ~270–600 W/m² (still wildly over observed), not 90–270. The number 90 looks like it inherits the c2 review's 'NH excess above mean = 93 W/m²' framing while the 270 number comes from a 'full NH-SH' framing. The two endpoints may not be apples-to-apples.
